original_file: Why Your Portfolio Isn’t the Problem (Yet)
email_subject: Why Your Portfolio Isn’t the Problem (Yet)
email_body:
It's Frankly UX



By Frankie Kastenbaum





By Frankie Kastenbaum



    

    

    Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation

Read on LinkedIn

https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/why-your-portfolio-isnt-problem-yet-frankie-kastenbaum-zas0e?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_01%3BRJxrkFdXRiuEIMjNw8R9JQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQEbAgAs2lKPUw&midSig=0RhbQA2cpDcc81&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_01-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_01-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-lczh4v~ml84lpr7~f0-null-null&eid=lczh4v-ml84lpr7-f0&otpToken=MTMwNzE3ZTExNDJlYzljNmIxMmIwZmViNDExOWU3YjY4ZWNiZDU0MTk5YWQ4NzZlN2JjZjA5NjY0OTVhNThmM2ZmZDNkZGU5NDBkNGJjODI1N2ZlZjFjMTgxNWM5YzU4YjIzNzVjNzBlZTlkNTRhZDYzMDUxZmU2LDEsMQ%3D%3D

  

If you’re breaking into UX and thinking “Maybe my portfolio just… isn’t good enough?” You’re not alone. And you’re probably not wrong in the way you think you are. But here’s the truth most people won’t say: For most junior designers, the issue isn’t talent or effort. It’s clarity. The uncomfortable truth about junior portfolios Most junior portfolios I review don’t make me think: “This person can’t design.” They make me think: “I’m not sure how this person thinks.” And that’s a huge difference. Hiring managers aren’t looking for perfection from juniors. They’re looking for signals: Can you explain why you made decisions? Can you connect research → insights → design choices? Can you communicate clearly? If I have to work too hard to understand your thinking, your portfolio starts working against you. The real problem (it’s not the UI) Here’s what’s usually going wrong: Case studies are too long and still somehow unclear Screens are shown without enough context Research is listed, not interpreted Decisions are implied, not explained So when someone says, “Your portfolio needs work,” what they often mean is: “I couldn’t follow your story.” That’s not a skill issue. That’s a storytelling issue. And storytelling is fixable. A quick self-check (do this in 10 minutes) Open one case study and ask yourself: Can someone understand the problem in 30 seconds or less? Is it obvious who this was for and why it mattered? Do my design decisions clearly tie back to user needs? Would someone who isn’t a designer understand this? If you hesitated on more than one of these, that’s your signal. One fix you can make this week Pick one case study (not all of them). Then: Cut 30–40% of the text Add one short “Why this matters” call out after your problem statement Rewrite one section as if you’re explaining it to a non-designer friend That alone will make your portfolio feel more senior. When your portfolio is the problem Now, to be fair, sometimes the portfolio is the issue. That’s usually when: There’s no clear problem being solved Projects feel more like Dribbble shots than real scenarios There’s no evidence of thinking or iteration If that’s you, don’t panic. It just means you need better framing, not a full restart. Final thought If you’re not hearing back, it doesn’t automatically mean: “I’m bad at UX.” More often, it means: “My portfolio isn’t telling my story clearly enough.” And that’s something you can fix without burning it all down.
